
---
title: "The Carbon Cost of Prosperity"
subtitle: "Examining the Development–Emission Tradeoff"
author: "Aiyan Warsi"
date: "July 16th, 2025"
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: ["default", "custom.css"]
    lib_dir: libs
    nature:
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
---

```{r themer, include=FALSE}

library(xaringanthemer)
file.exists("images/titlepic.jpg")
```

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE)
knitr::opts_knit$set(root.dir = "..")


library(htmlwidgets)
library(plotly)
library(ggplot2)
library(scales)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(maps)
library(sf)
library(rnaturalearth)
library(rnaturalearthdata)
library(skimr)
library(stargazer)
library(xaringan)
library(DT)
library(viridis)
library(ggrepel)
library(tibble)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
library(htmltools)

```

```{r data-loading, include=FALSE}

carbon <- read.csv("data/raw/owid-co2-data.csv")
ppp_data <- read.csv("data/raw/API_NY.GDP.PCAP.PP.CD_DS2_en_csv_v2_37774.csv", skip = 4)


ppp_long <- ppp_data %>%
  select(Country.Name, Country.Code , starts_with("X")) %>% 
  pivot_longer(cols = starts_with("X"), 
               names_to = "year", 
               values_to = "gdp_per_capita")
  
ppp_clean <- ppp_long %>% 
  mutate(year = as.numeric(gsub("X", "", year))) %>%
  rename(iso_code = Country.Code, 
         country = Country.Name) %>%
 
  mutate(country = case_when(
    country == "Brunei Darussalam" ~ "Brunei",
    TRUE ~ country
  )) 

combined_data <- carbon %>%
  left_join(ppp_clean, by = c("country", "year"))

```


# The planet is heating up fast, we're running out of time to act.


- Global temperatures have **risen by over 1.5°C since pre-industrial times**, with the steepest increases occurring in the last four decades. 


```{r temp_change,echo=FALSE, fig.width=11, fig.height=5}
library(plotly)
library(dplyr)

temp_anomaly <- read.csv("data/raw/temperature-anomaly.csv")

temp_anomaly_clean <- temp_anomaly %>%
  rename(
    Temperature_Anomaly = Global.average.temperature.anomaly.relative.to.1861.1890,
    Lower_Bound = Lower.bound.of.the.annual.temperature.anomaly..95..confidence.interval.,
    Upper_Bound = Upper.bound.of.the.annual.temperature.anomaly..95..confidence.interval.
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    hover_text = paste(
      "<b>Year:</b>", Year, "<br>",
      "<span style='color:red'>• Upper Bound:</span>", round(Upper_Bound, 2), "°C<br>",
      "<span style='color:black'>• Average:</span>", round(Temperature_Anomaly, 2), "°C<br>",
      "<span style='color:red'>• Lower Bound:</span>", round(Lower_Bound, 2), "°C"
    )
  )

# Create custom y-axis labels with °C
y_ticks <- seq(floor(min(temp_anomaly_clean$Lower_Bound, na.rm = TRUE)), 
              ceiling(max(temp_anomaly_clean$Upper_Bound, na.rm = TRUE)), 
              by = 0.2)
y_labels <- paste0(y_ticks, "°C")

# Custom x-axis labels (show every 20 years)
x_ticks <- seq(1860, 2020, by = 10)
x_labels <- ifelse(x_ticks %% 5 == 0, as.character(x_ticks), "")  # Show 1860, 1900, 1940, 1980, 2020

temp_anomaly_plot <- plot_ly(temp_anomaly_clean, x = ~Year, height = 400) %>%
  add_ribbons(
    ymin = ~Lower_Bound, 
    ymax = ~Upper_Bound, 
    name = 'Confidence Interval',
    line = list(color = 'transparent'),
    fillcolor = 'rgba(255, 0, 0, 0.2)',
    hoverinfo = 'none',
    showlegend = FALSE
  ) %>%
  add_lines(
    y = ~Temperature_Anomaly, 
    name = 'Average Anomaly',
    line = list(color = 'black', width = 2),
    hoverinfo = 'text',
    text = ~hover_text,
    showlegend = FALSE
  ) %>%
  add_segments(
    x = 1855, xend = 2025,
    y = 0, yend = 0,
    line = list(color = "gray", width = 1, dash = "solid"),
    name = "0°C Baseline",
    hoverinfo = "none"
  ) %>%
  layout(
    title = list(text = "Annual global temperature anomalies relative to the pre-industrial period", font = list(size = 16)),
    xaxis = list(
      title = "",
      range = c(1855, 2025),
      tickvals = x_ticks,
      ticktext = x_labels,
      gridcolor = 'lightgrey',
      showticklabels = TRUE
    ),
    yaxis = list(
      title = "",
      gridcolor = 'lightgrey',
      zeroline = FALSE,
      tickvals = y_ticks,
      ticktext = y_labels
    ),
    hovermode = 'x',
    showlegend = FALSE,
    plot_bgcolor = '#fff9e6',
    paper_bgcolor = '#fff9e6',
    margin = list(l = 50, r = 50, b = 80, t = 50, pad = 4),
    hoverlabel = list(
      bgcolor = "white",
      font = list(color = "black"),
      bordercolor = "lightgrey"
    ),
    annotations = list(
      list(
        x = 1, xref = "paper", xanchor = "right",
        y = -0.15, yref = "paper", yanchor = "top",
        text = "Source: Our World in Data (OWID)",
        showarrow = FALSE,
        font = list(size = 10, color = "gray")
      )
      )
  )

temp_anomaly_plot
```


---

<style>
.flexrow {
  display: flex;
  flex-wrap: wrap;
  justify-content: space-evenly;
  gap: 20px;
}
.flexrow > div {
  flex: 1 1 30%;
  min-width: 300px;
}
h3 {
  text-align: center;
  font-size: 18px;
  margin-top: 0.5em;
  margin-bottom: 0.3em;
}
</style>


#  22 countries out of 200 contribute 40% to global emissions
<!-- 1. Global Fuel Mix -->
<div>
<h3>Fuel Source Emissions</h3>
<div style="width:100%">

```{r fuel-dt, echo=FALSE, results='asis', message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(DT)
library(scales)

# Your original logic, with display formatting
global_fuel_mix <- carbon %>%
  filter(year == 2023, country == "World") %>%
  summarise(
    Coal    = coal_co2,
    Oil     = oil_co2,
    Gas     = gas_co2,
    Flaring = flaring_co2
  ) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = everything(),
               names_to = "Source",
               values_to = "CO2") %>%
  mutate(
    `CO2 Emissions (Mt)` = formatC(CO2, format = "f", big.mark = ",", digits = 1),
    `% of Total` = paste0(round(CO2 / sum(CO2) * 100, 1), "%")
  ) %>%
  select(Source, `CO2 Emissions (Mt)`, `% of Total`)

datatable(global_fuel_mix,
          options = list(dom = 't', pageLength = 5),
          rownames = FALSE)

```

</div> </div> <!-- 2. Emissions Inequality --> <div> <h3>Emissions Inequality</h3> <div style="width:100%"> 
```{r inequality_dt, echo=FALSE, results='asis'}
country23 <- carbon %>% 
  filter(year==2023, !is.na(iso_code), nchar(iso_code)==3,
         is.finite(co2_per_capita))

# rank countries by per‑capita emissions
country23 <- country23 %>% arrange(desc(co2_per_capita))
n_countries <- nrow(country23)
top_cut    <- ceiling(0.10 * n_countries)
bottom_cut <- floor(0.50 * n_countries)

ineq_tbl <- tibble(
  Group = c("Top 10 % countries", "Bottom 50 % countries"),
  `Number of countries` = c(top_cut, bottom_cut),
  `Share of CO₂ per cap` = c(
     round(sum(country23$co2_per_capita[1:top_cut]) /
           sum(country23$co2_per_capita) * 100, 1),
     round(sum(country23$co2_per_capita[(n_countries-bottom_cut+1):n_countries]) /
           sum(country23$co2_per_capita) * 100, 1))
)

datatable(ineq_tbl,
          colnames = c("Group", "No of Countries", "% of Global Per‑Cap CO₂"),
          options  = list(dom = 't', pageLength = 5),
          rownames = FALSE)

```
---
<!-- 3. Outliers -->
<div>
<div style="width:100%">

#Energy producing nations lead global emissions
```{r co2-outliers, echo=FALSE, results='asis'}
library(DT)

outliers_tbl <- bind_rows(
  tibble(Group = "Top 5 Emitters",
         carbon %>%
           filter(year == 2023, !is.na(iso_code), nchar(iso_code) == 3) %>%
           arrange(desc(co2_per_capita)) %>%
           select(Country = country, `CO₂ per capita (t)` = co2_per_capita) %>%
           head(5)),
  tibble(Group = "Bottom 5 Emitters",
         carbon %>%
           filter(year == 2023, !is.na(iso_code), nchar(iso_code) == 3,
                  co2_per_capita > 0) %>%
           arrange(co2_per_capita) %>%
           select(Country = country, `CO₂ per capita (t)` = co2_per_capita) %>%
           head(5))
)

datatable(outliers_tbl,
          options = list(dom = 't', pageLength = 10),
          rownames = FALSE)
```



---
#Global North dominates per capita Emissions


```{r share_10, include=FALSE}
# Assume carbon_country is already filtered for 2023 + valid CO2 per capita
carbon_country <- carbon %>%
  filter(
    year == 2023,
    !is.na(iso_code),
    nchar(iso_code) == 3,
    !is.na(co2),
    is.finite(co2)
  )

# Get top 10% of countries by co2_per_capita
cutoff <- quantile(carbon_country$co2, 0.9)

top_10 <- carbon_country %>%
  filter(co2 >= cutoff)

share_top_10 <- sum(top_10$co2) / sum(carbon_country$co2)
print(share_top_10)
```


```{r map-slide, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE, results='asis'}
library(plotly)
library(dplyr)

# --- Prepare data ---
carbon_map <- carbon %>% 
  filter(
    year == 2023,
    !is.na(iso_code), nchar(iso_code) == 3,
    !is.na(co2_per_capita), is.finite(co2_per_capita),
    !iso_code %in% c("OWID_WRL", "OWID_INT", "OWID_KOS")
  ) %>%
  mutate(z_log = log10(co2_per_capita))

# --- Custom color scale ---
viridis_orange_red <- list(
  list(0,   "#440154"),
  list(0.25,"#31688e"),
  list(0.50,"#35b779"),
  list(0.75,"orange"),
  list(1,   "red")
)

# --- Enlarged, centered plot ---
plot_ly(
  data        = carbon_map,
  type        = "choropleth",
  locations   = ~iso_code,
  z           = ~z_log,
  text        = ~paste(country, "<br>CO₂ per capita:", round(co2_per_capita, 2), "t"),
  hoverinfo   = "text",
  colorscale  = viridis_orange_red,
  marker      = list(line = list(color = "#fff9e6", width = 0.3)),
  colorbar    = list(
    title       = "tons / person",
    orientation = "h",
    len         = 0.9,
    x           = 0.5,
    xanchor     = "center",
    y           = 0.2,
    tickvals    = log10(c(0.1, 1, 5, 10, 20)),
    ticktext    = c("0.1", "1", "5", "10", "20")
  )
) %>%
layout(
  autosize = TRUE,
  height = 580,  # Increase map height
  width = 850 ,
  margin = list(l = 0, r = 0, t = 0, b = 0),
  geo = list(
    projection      = list(type = "equirectangular"),
    showframe       = FALSE,
    showcoastlines  = FALSE,
    bgcolor         = "#fdf6e3",
    center          = list(lat = 10, lon = 0), # optional: center globe
    fitbounds       = "locations"
  ),
  paper_bgcolor = "#fdf6e3",
  plot_bgcolor  = "#fdf6e3",
  annotations = list(
    list(
      text = "Small nations not rendered • Source: Our World in Data (2024)",
      x = 0.5,
      y = 0.05,
      xref = "paper",
      yref = "paper",
      showarrow = FALSE,
      font = list(size = 10, color = "gray")
    )
  )
)


``` 

```{r world v5, include=FALSE}
library(plotly)

# --- clean data ----------------------------------------------------------
carbon_map <- carbon %>%
  filter(year == 2023,
         !is.na(iso_code),
         nchar(iso_code) == 3,
         !is.na(co2_per_capita),
         is.finite(co2_per_capita),
         !iso_code %in% c("OWID_WRL", "OWID_INT", "OWID_KOS")) %>%
  mutate(
    z_log = log10(co2_per_capita)       # new variable for colouring
  )

# colour stops (Viridis‑ish → orange → red)
viridis_orange_red <- list(
  list(0,   "#440154"),   # dark purple
  list(0.25,"#31688e"),   # blue
  list(0.50,"#35b779"),   # green   (~1‑3 t)
  list(0.75,"orange"),    # orange  (~6‑10 t)
  list(1,   "red")        # red     (>15 t)
)

# map ---------------------------------------------------------------------
plot_ly(
  data        = carbon_map,
  type        = "choropleth",
  locations   = ~iso_code,
  z           = ~z_log,                   # <<< log colour variable
  text        = ~paste(country,
                       "<br>CO₂ per capita:",
                       round(co2_per_capita, 2), "tons"),
  hoverinfo   = "text",
  colorscale  = viridis_orange_red,
  marker      = list(line = list(color = "#fff9e6", width = 0.3)),
  colorbar    = list(
    title       = "Tons / person",
    # ticks in *original* units, but positioned by their log10 value
    tickvals    = log10(c(0.1, 1, 5, 10, 20)),
    ticktext    = c("0.1", "1", "5", "10", "20"),
    len         = 0.8,      # 90 % of figure height
    y           = 1,      # centred vertically
    thickness   = 20        # wider bar for readability
  )
) %>%
  layout(
    geo = list(showframe = FALSE,
               showcoastlines = FALSE,
               projection = list(type = "equirectangular"),
               bgcolor = "#fdf6e3"),      # cream around oceans if desired
    paper_bgcolor = "#fdf6e3",
    plot_bgcolor  = "#fdf6e3",
    margin = list(l = 0, r = 0, t = 40, b = 0),
    title  = list(text = "CO₂ per Capita by Country (2023)",
                  font = list(size = 16))
  )


```
---

# Per capita emissions rise as countries become more wealthy

```{r bigchungus, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE, results='asis'}
library(ggplot2)
library(plotly)
library(dplyr)

# Format pop labels
format_population <- function(pop) {
  case_when(
    pop >= 1e9 ~ paste0(round(pop/1e9, 1), "B"),
    pop >= 1e6 ~ paste0(round(pop/1e6, 1), "M"),
    pop >= 1e3 ~ paste0(round(pop/1e3, 1), "K"),
    TRUE ~ as.character(pop)
  )
}

# Filter and prep data
plot_data <- combined_data %>%
  filter(year == 2023, 
         !is.na(co2_per_capita), 
         !is.na(gdp_per_capita),
         gdp_per_capita > 0,
         co2_per_capita > 0,
         !country %in% c("World", "North America","Africa", "Asia", "Europe", 
                         "South America", "Oceania", "European Union (27)",
                         "High-income countries", "Upper-middle-income countries",
                         "Lower-middle-income countries", "Low-income countries"))

# Base ggplot
p <- ggplot(plot_data, 
            aes(x = gdp_per_capita, y = co2_per_capita)) +
  geom_point(aes(colour = country, size = population,
                 text = paste0(
                   "<b>", country, "</b><br>",
                   "CO₂ per capita: ", round(co2_per_capita, 1), " tonnes<br>",
                   "Population: ", format_population(population), "<br>",
                   "GDP per capita: $", format(round(gdp_per_capita), big.mark = ","))),
             alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", formula = y ~ x, 
              color = "red", se = FALSE, size = 1.2) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = scales::dollar_format()) +
  scale_y_log10(breaks = c(0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100)) +
  scale_size_continuous(range = c(1, 15),
                        breaks = c(1e6, 1e7, 1e8, 1e9),
                        labels = c("1M", "10M", "100M", "1B")) +
  labs(
    subtitle = "Log-log scale with regression line (red)",
    x = "GDP per capita (international-$ in 2023 prices)",
    y = "Annual CO₂ emissions (Tonnes per capita)",
    caption = "Data source: Our World in Data & World Bank"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

# Plotly version with styling
ggplotly(p, tooltip = "text") %>%
  layout(
    width = 900,
    height = 450,
    paper_bgcolor = "#fdf6e3",
    plot_bgcolor = "#fdf6e3",
    hoverlabel = list(
      bgcolor = "#fff9e6",
      font = list(size = 11, family = "Times New Roman", color = "black"),
      bordercolor = "gray"
    ),
    font = list(size = 12, family = "Times New Roman"),
    margin = list(l = 60, r = 20, t = 60, b = 60),
    annotations = list(
      list(
        text = "Note: Both axes on logarithmic scale",
        xref = "paper", yref = "paper",
        x = 0, y = -0.15, showarrow = FALSE,
        font = list(size = 10, color = "gray")
      )
    )
  )

```
---


#There is a clear <em>log-linear</em> relationship between emissions and income.
 

```{r regress, results='asis', include=FALSE}
library(stargazer)
library(broom)

regress_model <- combined_data %>%
  filter(year == 2023, 
         !is.na(co2_per_capita), 
         !is.na(gdp_per_capita),
         gdp_per_capita > 0,
         co2_per_capita > 0,
         !country %in% c("World", "North America","Africa", "Asia", "Europe", 
                        "South America", "Oceania", "European Union (27)",
                        "High-income countries", "Upper-middle-income countries",
                        "Lower-middle-income countries", "Low-income countries"))

# Calculate correlation coefficients
spearman_corr <- cor(regress_model$gdp_per_capita, regress_model$co2_per_capita, 
                    use = "complete.obs", method = "spearman")
kendall_corr <- cor(regress_model$gdp_per_capita, regress_model$co2_per_capita, 
                   use = "complete.obs", method = "kendall")
pearson_corr <- cor(regress_model$gdp_per_capita, regress_model$co2_per_capita, 
                   use = "complete.obs", method = "pearson")

# Fit linear model on log-transformed data
model <- lm(log10(co2_per_capita) ~ log10(gdp_per_capita), data = regress_model)

# For presentations, use HTML output with custom styling
stargazer(model, 
          type = "html",
          title = "Regression Results<br>Log(CO₂ per capita) vs Log(GDP per capita)",
          dep.var.labels = "Log(CO₂ per capita)",
          covariate.labels = "Log(GDP per capita)",
          star.cutoffs = c(0.05, 0.01, 0.001),
          notes = c("Note: *p<0.05; **p<0.01; ***p<0.001",
                   paste0("Spearman ρ = ", round(spearman_corr, 3), 
                         "; Kendall τ = ", round(kendall_corr, 3),
                         "; Pearson r = ", round(pearson_corr, 3))),
          notes.append = FALSE,
          header = FALSE,
          single.row = TRUE,  # Put coefficients and SEs on same row
          table.placement = "H",
          font.size = "small")
```

```{r table}
library(tibble)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
library(htmltools)

reg_tbl <- tibble(
  Variable = c("Log(GDP per capita)", "Constant"),
  Estimate = c("1.103", "-4.329"),
  `Std. Error` = c("0.042", "0.178"),
  `p-value` = c("<0.001 ***", "<0.001 ***")
)

reg_tbl_html <- paste0(
  "<div style='font-size: 24px; font-weight: bold; margin-bottom: 8px;'>Regression Coefficients</div>",
  kable(reg_tbl, "html", align = "lccc") %>%
    kable_styling(full_width = FALSE, font_size = 16, bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover")) %>%
    row_spec(0, bold = TRUE)
)

model_stats <- tibble(
  `Model Stat` = c("Observations", "R²", "Adjusted R²", 
                   "Residual Std. Error", "F Statistic"),
  Value = c("172", "0.804", "0.803", "0.278 (df = 170)", "697.880*** (df = 1; 170)")
)

model_tbl_html <- paste0(
  "<div style='font-size: 24px; font-weight: bold; margin: 30px 0 8px;'>Model Fit Statistics</div>",
  kable(model_stats, "html", align = "lc") %>%
    kable_styling(full_width = FALSE, font_size = 16, bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover")) %>%
    row_spec(0, bold = TRUE)
)


corr_tbl <- tibble(
  `Correlation Method` = c("Pearson’s (r)", "Spearman’s (ρ)", "Kendall’s (τ)"),
  Estimate = c("0.65", "0.876", "0.70"),
  `p-value` = c("<0.001", "<0.001", "<0.001")
)

corr_tbl_html <- paste0(
  "<div style='font-size: 24px; font-weight: bold; margin-bottom: 8px;'>Correlation Coefficients</div>",
  kable(corr_tbl, "html", align = "lcc") %>%
    kable_styling(full_width = FALSE, font_size = 16, bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover")) %>%
    row_spec(0, bold = TRUE)
)

HTML(
  paste0(
    "<div style='display: flex; justify-content: space-between;'>",
    
      
      "<div style='flex: 0.6; padding-right: 30px;'>",
        reg_tbl_html, "<br>", model_tbl_html,
        "<div style='font-size: 20px; margin-top: 25px;'> ",
        "log(CO₂ per capita) = 1.103 × log(GDP per capita) − 4.329</div>",
      "</div>",
      
      
      "<div style='flex: 0.4;'>",
        corr_tbl_html,
        "<div style='font-size: 20px; margin-top: 30px;'>",
        "<strong>Interpretation:</strong><ul>",
        "<li>GDP per capita and CO₂ per capita are strongly correlated; economic growth has historically required greenhouse gas emissions.</li>",
        "</ul></div>",
      "</div>",
      
    "</div>"
  )
)

```


---
# Residuals follow a Normal distribution


```{r shapiro,  results='asis'}

qq <- ggplot(mapping = aes(sample = residuals(model))) +
  stat_qq() + stat_qq_line(color = "red") +
  labs(title = "QQ Plot of Regression Residuals") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 16) +
  theme(
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = "#fdf6e3", color = NA),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = "#fdf6e3", color = NA),
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold")
  )

ggsave("images/qqplot.png", qq, width = 5, height = 4, bg = "transparent")

# Run Shapiro-Wilk test
shapiro_out <- shapiro.test(residuals(model))
shapiro_tbl <- tibble(
  Statistic = round(shapiro_out$statistic, 3),
  `p-value` = format(round(shapiro_out$p.value, 5), scientific = FALSE)
)

# Format table
shapiro_table_html <- shapiro_tbl %>%
  kable(format = "html", align = "c") %>%
  kable_styling(full_width = FALSE, font_size = 18, position = "center") %>%
  add_header_above(c("Shapiro–Wilk Test" = 2), bold = TRUE, font_size = 22) %>%
  row_spec(0, bold = TRUE)

# Output custom HTML layout
cat("
<div style='display: flex; justify-content: space-around; align-items: flex-start;'>
  <div style='flex: 1; text-align: center;'>
    <img src='images/qqplot.png' style='max-width: 100%; height: auto;' />
  </div>
  <div style='flex: 1; padding-left: 2em;'>", 
    shapiro_table_html, 
  "</div>
</div>

<div style='font-size:16pt; margin-top: 2em; line-height: 1.5;'>
  <strong>Key Points:</strong>
  <ul>
    <li>The residuals pass the Shapiro–Wilk test (<em>p</em> = ", shapiro_tbl$`p-value`, "), indicating no significant departure from normality.</li>
    <li>This supports the validity of the <em>log–log linear model</em>, as normality of residuals is an assumption for inference.</li>
  </ul>
</div>
")

```


```{r regress_1, results='asis' , include=FALSE}
library(stargazer)
library(broom)

regress_model <- combined_data %>%
  filter(year == 2023, 
         !is.na(co2_per_capita), 
         !is.na(gdp_per_capita),
         gdp_per_capita > 0,
         co2_per_capita > 0,
         !country %in% c("World", "North America","Africa", "Asia", "Europe", 
                        "South America", "Oceania", "European Union (27)",
                        "High-income countries", "Upper-middle-income countries",
                        "Lower-middle-income countries", "Low-income countries"))

# Fit linear model on log-transformed data
model <- lm(log10(co2_per_capita) ~ log10(gdp_per_capita), data = regress_model)

# For presentations, use HTML output with custom styling
stargazer(model, 
          type = "html",
          title = "Log(CO₂ per capita) vs Log(GDP per capita)",
          dep.var.labels = "Log(CO₂ per capita)",
          covariate.labels = "Log(GDP per capita)",
          star.cutoffs = c(0.05, 0.01, 0.001),
          notes = "Note: *p<0.05; **p<0.01; ***p<0.001",
          notes.append = FALSE,
          header = FALSE,
          single.row = TRUE,  # Put coefficients and SEs on same row
          table.placement = "H",
          font.size = "small")
```

---
# Political Will Is Key to Decoupling


- Higher GDP per capita is associated with higher CO₂ emissions per capita

- Economic growth is tightly linked to emissions: a **1% increase in GDP per capita** leads to a **1.103% increase in CO₂ emissions per capita**.


### Future Work 

- Examine broader drivers of emissions, including energy use, renewable energy share, industrial activity, and trade patterns.

- We need **robust, enforceable climate accords**—**there is no planet B**. Without stronger global cooperation, small nations like **Tuvalu** risk submersion, and the world faces irreversible damage.
---

# Data Sources and AI declaration

##Data Sources: 

<ul>
  <li>
    <strong>Our World in Data CO₂ and Energy Database:</strong>
    <a href="https://github.com/owid/co2-data" target="_blank">https://github.com/owid/co2-data</a>
  </li>
  <li>
    <strong>OWID Global Temperature Anomaly Data:</strong>
    <a href="https://ourworldindata.org/grapher/temperature-anomaly" target="_blank">https://ourworldindata.org/grapher/temperature-anomaly</a>
  </li>
  <li>
    <strong>World Bank GDP per capita (PPP-adjusted):</strong>
    <a href="https://data.worldbank.org/indicator/NY.GDP.PCAP.PP.CD" target="_blank">https://data.worldbank.org/indicator/NY.GDP.PCAP.PP.CD</a>
  </li>
  <li>
    <strong>IMF PPP GDP Estimates:</strong>
    <a href="https://www.imf.org/external/datamapper/PPPPC@WEO/OEMDC/ADVEC/WEOWORLD" target="_blank">https://www.imf.org/external/datamapper/PPPPC@WEO/OEMDC/ADVEC/WEOWORLD</a>
  </li>
</ul>


## AI declaration:
This presentation was prepared by Aiyan Warsi. OpenAI’s ChatGPT was used to assist with the following:

- Converting static ggplots into interactive HTML widgets using plotly.

- Styling the HTML slides, including layout, font formatting, and customizing visual elements to ensure a clean and professional design.

- Formatting support for specific slide elements and CSS to improve visual clarity and structure.
